speaker: patient
text: Hi, Doctor. I’ve been feeling a bit off lately—mostly dizzy and tired, especially in the mornings.

speaker: doctor
text: Thanks for coming in. Let’s start with your vitals—I just measured your blood pressure: systolic is 120 mmHg.

speaker: patient
text: That sounds normal, right? My usual readings at home have been around there.

speaker: doctor
text: Yes, that’s reassuring. You’re currently on Lisinopril 20mg once daily—we started that last month for blood pressure control and cardiac protection.

speaker: doctor
text: Your diastolic pressure today is 62 mmHg—on the lower side, but not critically so yet.

speaker: patient
text: Oh—I’ve had some nausea this morning, actually. Not severe, but it’s been happening a few times this week.

speaker: doctor
text: Given your history and today’s findings, I’m confirming your primary diagnosis as Coronary Artery Disease. That guides our treatment decisions.

speaker: doctor
text: Your heart rate is 51 bpm—mildly bradycardic, which we’ll monitor closely, especially with your current meds.

speaker: patient
text: I did notice my pulse felt slow sometimes—I’ve been checking it with my smartwatch.

speaker: doctor
text: Good awareness. Let’s repeat the BP now—sometimes position or timing affects it. Are you seated comfortably?

speaker: doctor
text: This time, your diastolic pressure is 78 mmHg—higher than the earlier reading of 62. That’s more in line with what we’d expect on Lisinopril.

speaker: patient
text: That makes sense—I stood up quickly before the first reading. Maybe that threw it off.

speaker: doctor
text: Exactly—orthostatic changes can influence this. Let’s check your heart rate again in a moment.

speaker: doctor
text: Now your heart rate is 50 bpm—slightly lower than the 51 we saw earlier. We’ll keep an eye on trends, especially with your symptoms.

speaker: patient
text: I’ve also noticed mild lightheadedness when standing—could that be related to the low heart rate or the medication?

speaker: doctor
text: It could be. Given the persistent bradycardia and new-onset nausea, I’m going to switch you from Lisinopril to Amlodipine 5mg daily—it’s less likely to affect heart rate and still protects your coronary arteries.

speaker: patient
text: Okay, I’ll start that tomorrow. Should I stop the Lisinopril cold turkey?

speaker: doctor
text: Yes—discontinue Lisinopril tonight and begin Amlodipine tomorrow morning. Monitor for swelling or flushing, and let me know if dizziness worsens.

speaker: patient
text: Will do. By the way, I checked my pulse again just now—it’s back to 51.

speaker: patient
text: Same as that number you got at the start—the 51 bpm.
